questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman is seen in the pre-operative assessment clinic two weeks before an elective laparoscopic cholecystectomy for symptomatic gallstones. She is asymptomatic. Blood tests show: bilirubin 25 µmol/L (normal <21), ALP 180 U/L (30-130), GGT 90 U/L (5-55), ALT 45 U/L (10-40). What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The raised ALP and GGT with mild hyperbilirubinaemia suggest cholestasis from a common bile duct stone, even without symptoms. Gilbert's syndrome causes isolated unconjugated hyperbilirubinaemia. Pancreatitis, cholecystitis, and cholangitis typically present with pain and systemic features.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man with known diverticulosis is scheduled for an elective sigmoid colectomy due to previous episodes of diverticulitis. At pre-operative assessment, he reports a two-day history of left lower quadrant pain and fever (37.9°C). Examination reveals tenderness in the left iliac fossa without guarding. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Localised left iliac fossa pain with fever and tenderness in a patient with known diverticulosis indicates acute diverticulitis. Diverticular bleeding presents with rectal bleeding without pain. IBS does not cause fever. Carcinoma is typically painless. Mesenteric ischaemia causes diffuse severe pain out of proportion to tenderness.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man with a history of gallstones is admitted with severe epigastric pain radiating to the back, vomiting, and fever. On examination, he has epigastric tenderness and reduced bowel sounds. Bloods show raised amylase (1200 U/L), WCC 15, CRP 250. He is being assessed for emergency cholecystectomy. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Severe epigastric pain radiating to the back, vomiting, and markedly raised amylase are classic for acute pancreatitis. Cholecystitis pain is right upper quadrant, not typically radiating to back. Cholangitis has Charcot's triad (jaundice, fever, RUQ pain). Perforated ulcer causes board-like rigidity. Mesenteric ischaemia gives diffuse pain with risk factors.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old woman is seen in the pre-operative assessment clinic for elective repair of a small ventral hernia. She reports that over the past week the hernia has become more painful and cannot be reduced. On examination, there is a tender, irreducible mass in the midline, with overlying erythema. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The irreducible, tender hernia with erythema but without signs of systemic toxicity (e.g., fever, severe pain, peritonitis) suggests incarceration rather than strangulation. Strangulation would present with severe pain, fever, and possibly shock. Richter's hernia involves only part of bowel wall, often less prominent. Omental fat is usually less tender.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old pregnant woman (28 weeks gestation) with known gallstones presents for pre-operative assessment for laparoscopic cholecystectomy. She reports intermittent right upper quadrant pain after meals. Examination is unremarkable. Blood pressure 110/70 mmHg, urine protein negative. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Postprandial right upper quadrant pain without tenderness or systemic symptoms is classic for biliary colic due to gallstones. Acute cholecystitis would have tenderness and fever. Pre-eclampsia and HELLP syndrome involve hypertension and/or proteinuria, which are absent. Choledocholithiasis typically causes jaundice.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old woman is listed for elective laparoscopic cholecystectomy for symptomatic gallstones. Routine pre-operative blood tests show haemoglobin 105 g/L (normal >120), MCV 78 fL (normal 80–100). She has no other symptoms. What is the single most appropriate investigation to determine the cause of her anaemia?
options: Serum ferritin, iron, and total iron-binding capacity
questions.explanation: Microcytic anaemia is most commonly due to iron deficiency. Ferritin is the best confirmatory test; a low ferritin confirms iron deficiency. B12/folate are for macrocytic anaemia, and haemoglobin electrophoresis is for haemoglobinopathies, which are less likely in this demographic.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old man is scheduled for elective right hemicolectomy for colon cancer. Pre-operative LFTs show elevated ALP 250 U/L (normal 30–130) with normal ALT and bilirubin. He has no abdominal pain or jaundice. What is the single most appropriate investigation to confirm the presence of liver metastases?
options: MRI of the liver with contrast
Contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen and pelvis
questions.explanation: For colorectal cancer staging, contrast-enhanced CT of chest/abdomen/pelvis is the first-line investigation for liver metastases. Ultrasound has lower sensitivity for small lesions; MRI is reserved for characterising indeterminate lesions. PET-CT is not routine for initial staging, and biopsy is avoided unless imaging is equivocal.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man with known COPD (FEV1 40% predicted) is listed for elective inguinal hernia repair. He is short of breath on climbing one flight of stairs. Pre-operative oxygen saturation is 95% on room air. What is the single most appropriate additional pre-operative investigation?
questions.explanation: NICE guidelines recommend arterial blood gas (ABG) in patients with severe COPD (FEV1 <50% predicted) to assess for hypercapnia or hypoxia, which may require optimisation before surgery. Chest X-ray is low yield, and CT chest is not routinely needed. Echocardiogram is only if cardiac disease is suspected.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old woman is listed for elective mastectomy for breast cancer. Pre-operative ECG shows a corrected QT interval (QTc) of 480 ms (normal < 450 ms). She has no cardiac symptoms and takes no medications known to prolong QT. What is the single most appropriate next investigation?
options: Review of all current medications and supplements
questions.explanation: Electrolyte abnormalities (hypokalaemia, hypocalcaemia, hypomagnesaemia) are common reversible causes of prolonged QT. Checking serum levels is the first step; correction may normalise the QT. Repeat ECG alone is insufficient, and echocardiogram/Holter are not first line. Medication review is appropriate but electrolyte check is more urgent and diagnostic.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old man with a BMI of 45 kg/m² is listed for laparoscopic gastric banding. On pre-operative assessment, his oxygen saturation is 94% on room air. He reports daytime somnolence but no witnessed apnoeas. What is the single most appropriate investigation to confirm a diagnosis of obesity hypoventilation syndrome (OHS)?
questions.explanation: OHS is defined by hypercapnia (PaCO₂ > 6.0 kPa) in the absence of other causes. ABG is diagnostic. Overnight oximetry or polysomnography can diagnose sleep apnoea but not OHS specifically. Pulmonary function tests help exclude other lung disease but are not confirmatory for OHS. Capnography is not used in this context.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man with atrial fibrillation (CHA2DS2-VASc score 4) on warfarin is scheduled for elective laparoscopic cholecystectomy. His current INR is 2.5. What is the most appropriate perioperative management of his anticoagulation?
options: Bridge with unfractionated heparin infusion until surgery
Continue warfarin throughout the perioperative period
Switch warfarin to a direct oral anticoagulant (DOAC) for the perioperative period
Stop warfarin 5 days before surgery and start low-molecular-weight heparin (LMWH) when INR < 2
Stop warfarin 5 days before surgery and administer oral vitamin K
questions.explanation: NICE recommends bridging with LMWH for patients at high thromboembolic risk (CHA2DS2-VASc ≥2) undergoing surgery with moderate/high bleeding risk. Continuing warfarin (option A) risks bleeding; vitamin K (C) reverses without bridging; UFH (D) is less convenient; switching to DOAC (E) is not standard perioperative management.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman with heavy menstrual bleeding and iron deficiency anaemia (Hb 95 g/L, MCV 78 fL, ferritin 12 µg/L) is scheduled for an elective hysterectomy in 4 weeks. She has no comorbidities. What is the most appropriate first-line treatment for her anaemia?
options: Oral ferrous sulphate 200 mg three times daily
questions.explanation: For mild-to-moderate iron deficiency anaemia with adequate time before surgery, oral iron is first-line (NICE). IV iron (B) is reserved for intolerance or severe disease; transfusion (C) is for haemodynamic instability or severe anaemia; erythropoietin (D) is not indicated; folic acid (E) treats megaloblastic anaemia, not iron deficiency.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes on metformin 1 g twice daily and gliclazide 160 mg twice daily is undergoing an elective right hemicolectomy. Her preoperative HbA1c is 58 mmol/mol. What is the most appropriate management of her oral hypoglycaemic agents on the day of surgery?
options: Substitute both with a variable-rate intravenous insulin infusion
questions.explanation: For major surgery, NICE recommends omitting both sulfonylureas (due to hypoglycaemia risk) and metformin (due to risk of lactic acidosis, especially if renal function may be affected). Continuing either (A, B, C) is unsafe. An insulin infusion (E) is reserved for poorly controlled diabetes or major surgery but here the first step is to omit oral agents and monitor.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with asthma who has been taking prednisolone 10 mg daily for over 2 years is scheduled for an elective laparoscopic cholecystectomy. He is otherwise well. What is the most appropriate perioperative corticosteroid management?
options: Give intravenous hydrocortisone 50 mg at induction and then 25 mg 8-hourly for 24 hours
Switch prednisolone to oral hydrocortisone 20 mg in the morning and 10 mg in the evening
Continue the usual prednisolone 10 mg daily
No change in corticosteroid therapy is required
questions.explanation: Long-term steroid use (>2 weeks) can suppress the HPA axis; for moderate-risk surgery (laparoscopic cholecystectomy), NICE recommends a stress dose of IV hydrocortisone perioperatively. Continuing the same dose (B) may be insufficient; stopping (A) risks adrenal crisis; switching to oral HC (D) doesn't cover the stress adequately; no change (E) is incorrect.

questions.stem: A 75-year-old man with stable angina and no history of bleeding is on aspirin 75 mg daily for secondary prevention. He is scheduled for an elective inguinal hernia repair under general anaesthesia. What is the most appropriate management of aspirin?
options: Replace aspirin with clopidogrel 75 mg daily
questions.explanation: Inguinal hernia repair is a low bleeding risk procedure; NICE recommends continuing aspirin to avoid increasing cardiac risk. Stopping (B, C) is reserved for high bleeding risk surgery; switching to clopidogrel (D) is not indicated; permanent discontinuation (E) is inappropriate for secondary prevention.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man is seen in the pre-operative assessment clinic for an elective inguinal hernia repair. He is asymptomatic. His pulse is irregular, and ECG shows atrial fibrillation with a ventricular rate of 130 bpm. He is not on any anticoagulation or rate control medication. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
questions.explanation: In asymptomatic atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response, the first step is rate control with a beta-blocker (e.g., bisoprolol) to reduce perioperative risk. Referral to cardiology is secondary, and digoxin is no longer first-line for rate control. Anticoagulation is needed but not immediately before rate control.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old woman on warfarin for recurrent DVT presents with acute epigastric pain and peritonism. CT shows a perforated duodenal ulcer. She needs emergency laparotomy. Her INR is 3.2. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Give 2 units of fresh frozen plasma
Give 5 mg of vitamin K intravenously
questions.explanation: For emergency surgery in a patient on warfarin with elevated INR, NICE recommends prothrombin complex concentrate (PCC) as the fastest and most effective reversal agent. FFP is less effective and requires larger volumes; vitamin K alone takes hours; TEG is not first-line; surgery cannot be cancelled for a perforated viscus.

questions.stem: An 82-year-old woman with a fractured neck of femur is awaiting surgery. Her preoperative blood tests show haemoglobin 90 g/L, MCV 78 fL, ferritin 12 μg/L. She is asymptomatic and haemodynamically stable. She is known to have ischaemic heart disease. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Transfuse 2 units of packed red cells
questions.explanation: In patients with hip fracture and ischaemic heart disease, NICE recommends a higher transfusion threshold (Hb < 100 g/L) to reduce cardiac complications. Immediate transfusion is indicated before urgent surgery. Oral iron is too slow, and proceeding without correction increases risk.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old man with severe COPD (FEV1 40% predicted) is listed for an elective laparoscopic cholecystectomy. He uses home oxygen intermittently. On pre-operative assessment, his oxygen saturations are 94% on air. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Refer to anaesthetics for high-risk assessment
Proceed with surgery under spinal anaesthesia
questions.explanation: For severe COPD with FEV1 < 50%, NICE recommends arterial blood gas analysis to detect compensated hypercapnia and guide perioperative management. Spinal anaesthesia is inappropriate for laparoscopic surgery; referral to anaesthetics is important but ABG should be done first to inform that referral.

questions.stem: A 52-year-old woman with a history of acute MI 6 weeks ago (treated with primary PCI and dual antiplatelet therapy: aspirin and ticagrelor) is now scheduled for an elective inguinal hernia repair. She is haemodynamically stable with no ongoing chest pain. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Continue dual antiplatelet therapy and proceed
Postpone surgery until 6 months after the MI
Stop ticagrelor 5 days before surgery and continue aspirin
questions.explanation: NICE and ESC guidelines recommend delaying elective non-cardiac surgery for at least 6 months after an MI due to high perioperative cardiac risk. Stopping antiplatelets prematurely risks stent thrombosis; proceeding with surgery despite recent MI is dangerous. Stress echo is not the immediate priority.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man presents to ED with sudden severe epigastric pain, board-like rigidity, and hypotension. An erect chest X-ray is performed. Which single radiological finding is most diagnostic of the underlying life-threatening condition?
questions.explanation: Pneumoperitoneum on erect CXR indicates perforated viscus (e.g., peptic ulcer), which requires immediate surgical intervention. Pleural effusion and widened mediastinum are not typical for this presentation.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man with CKD stage 4 (eGFR 20 mL/min) is listed for elective open inguinal hernia repair. His latest bloods show: Na+ 136, K+ 6.5, urea 32, Cr 350. Which single finding is a red flag that requires urgent correction before proceeding?
questions.explanation: Severe hyperkalaemia (>6.0 mmol/L) carries risk of cardiac arrhythmia; surgery should be delayed and K+ lowered urgently. While high urea and Cr indicate renal impairment, they are not as immediately life-threatening in this context.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man undergoes elective open repair of an infrarenal abdominal aortic aneurysm (AAA). On day 1 post-operatively, he complains of sudden severe back pain and becomes hypotensive. His left leg is cold, mottled, and pulses are absent. Which single clinical sign is most consistent with the life-threatening complication?
questions.explanation: Absent femoral pulse with cold, mottled leg indicates acute limb ischaemia, likely from embolic occlusion of the graft limb; this is a surgical emergency requiring immediate exploration.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old woman with known gallstones awaits elective laparoscopic cholecystectomy. During pre-operative assessment, you note she has a history of brief episodes of jaundice lasting a few days. Current LFTs and bilirubin are normal. Which single feature in her history is a red flag for a potential common bile duct stone?
questions.explanation: A history of jaundice (even if resolved) suggests possible intermittent CBD obstruction; MRCP is indicated to exclude a retained stone before cholecystectomy.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man presents with a 3-month history of intermittent crampy abdominal pain, bloating, and altered bowel habit (constipation alternating with diarrhoea). On abdominal examination, you feel a firm, non-tender mass in the left iliac fossa. Which single finding is the strongest red flag for colorectal cancer requiring urgent referral?
questions.explanation: A palpable mass is a classic red flag for colonic malignancy; while occult blood and anaemia are also concerning, a mass has high specificity for a tumour and mandates urgent colonoscopy.
